worth_it_explanation: Exclusions are a configuration change in Ads Manager, so the checks are quick and the fix is fast. The payoff is twofold. Excluding existing customers from prospecting keeps acquisition spend pointed at people who have not bought, and separating prospecting from retargeting keeps an advertiser's own ad sets out of the same auction, where Meta enters only the ad set with the stronger history and holds the others back. Both gaps show up as wasted reach rather than an error, so they go unnoticed until someone reads the audience overlap and auction overlap reports.
intro: Audience exclusions decide who an ad set is allowed to skip. A prospecting ad set that does not exclude existing customers spends acquisition budget on people who have already bought. Two ad sets from the same advertiser that target similar people land in the same auction, where Meta runs only one of them and prevents the rest from competing. These checks confirm that converters and customers are excluded from prospecting, that prospecting and retargeting do not overlap in the auction, and that a suppression list keeps recent buyers out of the audiences where they do not belong.

audit_checks.title: Existing customers and recent converters are not excluded from prospecting ad sets
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A prospecting ad set with no customer exclusion spends acquisition budget reaching people who have already bought. The conversions those impressions drive look like new business in reporting, which can overstate prospecting efficiency and steer budget toward an audience that was going to convert anyway.

educate.base:
A prospecting campaign is meant to reach people who do not yet know the business. To hold that line, the prospecting ad sets have to exclude the people who are already customers. In Meta Ads, that exclusion is built from a Custom Audience. A customer list Custom Audience is matched from hashed first-party data the advertiser uploads, such as an email address, and a website Custom Audience is built from purchase events the dataset records. Either one can be added to an ad set as an exclusion so the people in it are skipped.

When the exclusion is missing, the prospecting ad set is free to deliver to existing customers. The impressions and the conversions they produce get attributed to prospecting, so the campaign looks more efficient than it is at finding new buyers. The budget that was meant to grow the customer base is partly spent reaching the customer base it already has.

Advantage+ audience changes the shape of this, not the need for it. Advantage+ treats the audience as a suggestion rather than a hard boundary, so the prospecting and exclusion inputs guide delivery instead of fencing it. Even there, an exclusion Custom Audience tells the system which people to hold back, which is why the customer list still belongs on a prospecting campaign.

A few signs point to a missing customer exclusion:
  • The prospecting ad set lists no Custom Audience under its exclusions
  • A customer list or purchaser Custom Audience exists in the account but is not applied to any prospecting ad set
  • The same conversion events appear in both prospecting and retargeting reporting for the same people
You may want to confirm that a current customer or purchaser Custom Audience exists and that it is applied as an exclusion on every prospecting ad set. An audience that is not refreshed goes stale, so a list uploaded once and never updated misses recent buyers.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the cleanest exclusion source is a purchaser Custom Audience built from the dataset's purchase event. It updates as orders come in rather than depending on a manual upload. You may want to confirm a prospecting ad set excludes recent purchasers, and consider whether the window matches the buying cycle. A 30-day exclusion suits a frequent repeat purchase, while a longer window suits a product people buy once.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the equivalent of a customer is a person who already submitted the form or became a qualified lead, so the exclusion is built from a lead event or an uploaded list of known contacts. You may want to confirm the prospecting ad set excludes existing leads, otherwise the campaign pays to reach people who are already in the pipeline.

investigate.intro: Open each prospecting ad set and confirm it excludes a current customer or purchaser Custom Audience, and that the audience is fresh rather than a one-time upload.

ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: In Ads Manager, filter to the prospecting campaigns and open an ad set's editing view.
In the audience section, read the exclusions. Note whether a customer or purchaser Custom Audience is listed, or whether the exclusions are empty.
Repeat for each prospecting ad set so a single excluded ad set does not hide gaps in the others.

audiences_table.label: Audiences table
steps: Open 'Audiences' in the account and find the customer or purchaser Custom Audience used for exclusions.
Check the audience size and its availability or last-updated status. A customer list that was uploaded once and never refreshed will not contain recent buyers.
Confirm the audience that exists is the one actually applied as an exclusion on the prospecting ad sets, not a similarly named audience that is unused.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per prospecting ad set, whether a current customer or purchaser Custom Audience is applied as an exclusion and whether that audience is kept current. A prospecting ad set that excludes a fresh customer audience passes. A prospecting ad set with no customer exclusion, or one that excludes a stale list that misses recent buyers, is a fail recorded with the ad set name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record, for each prospecting ad set, whether a current customer exclusion is applied above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s prospecting ad sets do not exclude existing customers and recent converters. A prospecting campaign is meant to reach new buyers, and without a customer exclusion it spends acquisition budget reaching people who have already purchased, which overstates how efficiently prospecting finds new business. {{voice_recommend}} building a current customer or purchaser Custom Audience from uploaded first-party data or the dataset's purchase event, applying it as an exclusion on every prospecting ad set, and keeping it refreshed so it captures recent buyers. After the change, confirm in Ads Manager that each prospecting ad set lists the customer audience under its exclusions.

audit_checks.title: Prospecting and retargeting ad sets overlap and compete in the same auction
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When prospecting and retargeting ad sets target the same people, they enter the same auction. Meta runs only the ad set with the stronger history and holds the others back, so spend concentrates in one ad set while the others under-deliver. The advertiser pays to compete against itself, which can raise costs and starve ad sets that would otherwise reach distinct audiences.

educate.base:
When two ad sets from the same advertiser target similar people, they end up eligible for the same auction. Meta does not let an advertiser bid against itself there. It enters the ad set with the stronger performance history and prevents the others from competing, so one ad set wins the impression and the rest sit out. This is auction overlap, and it is the mechanism behind prospecting and retargeting interfering with each other when their audiences are not separated.

The setup that causes it is a retargeting ad set built from people who visited the site or engaged with the brand, running alongside a prospecting ad set that does not exclude those same people. The prospecting audience then contains the retargeting audience inside it. Because the two ad sets reach overlapping people, delivery consolidates into whichever ad set has the better history, and budget that was assigned to the other ad set goes underspent rather than reaching a separate group.

The fix is an exclusion in one direction. The prospecting ad set excludes the retargeting Custom Audience, so the two ad sets stop competing for the same people. Meta reports this through two views. The audience overlap tool compares the people in two saved audiences, and the auction overlap module inside the Inspect tool shows whether an active ad set's delivery is being held back by competition with another of the advertiser's own ad sets.

A few signs point to prospecting and retargeting overlapping in the auction:
  • A prospecting ad set does not exclude the website or engagement Custom Audience that a retargeting ad set targets
  • The Inspect tool reports auction overlap on an ad set, with delivery declining as overlap rises
  • One ad set in a pair spends its budget while the other under-delivers despite an active status
You may want to compare the prospecting and retargeting audiences in the overlap tool, then add the retargeting Custom Audience as an exclusion on the prospecting ad set so each ad set reaches a distinct group.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, retargeting ad sets are usually built from product-view or add-to-cart events while prospecting reaches cold traffic, and the two overlap when the prospecting ad set does not exclude recent site visitors. You may want to exclude the website Custom Audience that feeds retargeting from the prospecting ad set, so a shopper who just browsed is reached by retargeting rather than by both at once.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, retargeting often reaches people who started but did not finish the form, while prospecting reaches new traffic. They overlap when the prospecting ad set does not exclude prior site visitors. You may want to exclude the visitor or form-abandon Custom Audience from the prospecting ad set so the two stages do not compete for the same person.

investigate.intro: Compare the prospecting and retargeting audiences for overlap, then check the Inspect tool for auction overlap on the affected ad sets.

audience_overlap_tool.label: Audience overlap tool
steps: Open 'Audiences', select the prospecting audience and the retargeting Custom Audience, and choose 'Show audience overlap'.
Read the overlap percentage. A high overlap means the two ad sets are reaching many of the same people and are likely competing in the auction.
Note whether the prospecting ad set already excludes the retargeting audience. If it does not, the overlap is the gap to close.

inspect_tool.label: Inspect tool
steps: Open an active ad set, select 'Inspect', and find the auction overlap module.
Read whether delivery is being held back by competition with another of the account's ad sets, and whether overlap has risen as performance fell.
Identify which ad set in the pair is winning delivery and which is sitting out, so the exclusion can be applied to the prospecting side.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per prospecting and retargeting pair, the audience overlap percentage, whether the prospecting ad set excludes the retargeting Custom Audience, and any auction overlap the Inspect tool reports. A pair where the prospecting ad set excludes the retargeting audience and the Inspect tool shows no delivery impact passes. A pair with high overlap, no exclusion, and auction overlap holding back delivery is a fail recorded with the ad set names.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the overlap percentage and the exclusion status for each prospecting and retargeting pair above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s prospecting and retargeting ad sets overlap and compete in the same auction. When two ad sets target the same people, Meta runs only the one with the stronger history and holds the others back, so spend concentrates in one ad set while the other under-delivers and the account pays to compete against itself. {{voice_recommend}} comparing the audiences in the overlap tool, then excluding the retargeting Custom Audience from the prospecting ad set so each reaches a distinct group. After the change, check the auction overlap module in the Inspect tool to confirm delivery is no longer held back by the account's own ad sets.

audit_checks.title: No suppression list keeps recent buyers out of acquisition and retargeting audiences
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Without a suppression list, recent buyers stay eligible for both acquisition and retargeting, so the account keeps paying to reach people who already converted. The waste is modest per impression but steady, and it inflates the conversions credited to campaigns that were reaching customers rather than finding or recovering them.

educate.base:
A suppression list is a Custom Audience of people the advertiser wants held out of delivery, applied as an exclusion across the ad sets where those people do not belong. The most useful one is a recent-buyer suppression list. A person who bought yesterday should not be in a prospecting audience meant for new customers, and depending on the product they should not be in a retargeting audience pushing them toward a purchase they already made.

The suppression list is built the same way as any exclusion audience. It can come from an uploaded customer list, refreshed as new orders arrive, or from a purchaser Custom Audience the dataset maintains from the purchase event. The difference from a one-off exclusion is that a suppression list is applied consistently across the campaigns that should skip recent buyers, rather than added to a single ad set and forgotten.

Without it, recent buyers stay eligible everywhere. Acquisition campaigns reach them and count their next action as new business, and retargeting campaigns keep spending on people who already converted. The amount per person is small, so the cost is easy to miss in a campaign-level report and shows up only when someone checks whether buyers are still being served.

A few signs point to a missing suppression list:
  • No recent-buyer Custom Audience is applied as an exclusion across acquisition and retargeting ad sets
  • A purchaser audience exists but is attached to only one ad set rather than the campaigns that should all skip buyers
  • The exclusion audience in use was uploaded once and is not refreshed as new orders arrive
You may want to confirm a recent-buyer Custom Audience exists, that it refreshes as orders come in, and that it is applied as an exclusion everywhere recent buyers should be skipped. The right exclusion window depends on the buying cycle, so a product people rebuy often needs a shorter window than one they buy once.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, the suppression list is naturally a purchaser Custom Audience built from the dataset's purchase event, which refreshes as orders post. You may want to confirm it is applied across acquisition and any retargeting that should skip buyers, and set the window to the repurchase cycle. A consumable people rebuy in weeks needs a short window, while a one-time purchase needs a longer one so the buyer is not retargeted for the same item.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, the suppression list is the set of people who already converted into a lead or a sale, built from a lead event or an uploaded contact list. You may want to apply it as an exclusion across acquisition campaigns so the account stops paying to generate a lead from someone who is already in the pipeline.

investigate.intro: Confirm a recent-buyer Custom Audience exists, that it stays current, and that it is applied as an exclusion across the acquisition and retargeting ad sets that should skip buyers.

audiences_table.label: Audiences table
steps: Open 'Audiences' and look for a recent-buyer or purchaser Custom Audience built from an uploaded list or the purchase event.
Check its size and last-updated or availability status to confirm it refreshes rather than holding a one-time snapshot.
Note the exclusion window the audience represents and whether it fits the product's repurchase cycle.

ads_manager.label: Meta Ads Manager
steps: Open the acquisition and retargeting ad sets that should skip recent buyers and read their exclusions.
Confirm the recent-buyer Custom Audience is applied as an exclusion on each of them, not just on one ad set.
Record any ad set that should skip buyers but lists no recent-buyer exclusion.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether a recent-buyer Custom Audience exists, whether it is kept current, and whether it is applied as an exclusion across the acquisition and retargeting ad sets that should skip buyers. A current suppression list applied consistently across those ad sets passes. A missing list, a stale list, or a list applied to only some of the ad sets that should skip buyers is a fail recorded with the affected ad set names.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a current recent-buyer suppression list is applied across the relevant ad sets above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has no suppression list keeping recent buyers out of its acquisition and retargeting audiences. Without one, people who already purchased stay eligible for delivery, so acquisition campaigns count their next action as new business and retargeting keeps spending on people who already converted. {{voice_recommend}} building a recent-buyer Custom Audience from the purchase event or an uploaded customer list, refreshing it as orders arrive, applying it as an exclusion across the campaigns that should skip buyers, and setting the window to the product's repurchase cycle. After the change, confirm in Ads Manager that the suppression audience appears under the exclusions on each of those ad sets.
